1: The story opens with ʾîš ʾeḥāḏ ('a certain man') — the same formula as Judg 13:2 (Manoah's introduction) and Ruth 1:1 (Elimelech's introduction). The formula marks an ordinary Israelite whose family will become the vehicle for YHWH's extraordinary purpose. Rāmātayim (H7436, dual of rāmāh, heights) — two heights; ṣôpîm (watchers/Zuphites). Elkanah (ʾelqānāh H511) means 'God has created/acquired' — a theophoric name with ʾēl and qānāh (the root of Gen 14:19: 'God Most High, creator of heaven and earth'). His four-generation genealogy roots him in the clan of Zuph of Ephraim (the land of Zuph reappears in 1 Sam 9:5).
2: The two-wife structure: Ḥannāh (H2584, grace/favor — from ḥēn) and Pĕninnāh (H6444, possibly from pənînîm, corals/rubies — Prov 3:15; 8:11 use the word for what wisdom exceeds in value). ʿāqārāh (H6135, barren) — the theologically loaded term for a woman YHWH has not opened. The OT's barren-matriarch pattern: Sarah (Gen 11:30) → Rebekah (Gen 25:21) → Rachel (Gen 29:31) → Samson's mother (Judg 13:2) → Hannah. Each barrenness is YHWH's withholding before miraculous provision of a child with a covenantal role. The pattern marks Samuel as one in that line before his birth is narrated.
3: YHWH Ṣəḇāʾôt (LORD of Armies/Hosts) — this divine title appears with new frequency in Samuel; ṣāḇāʾ (H6635, army/host) denotes earthly armies, the heavenly angelic forces, and the stars (the host of heaven). The title asserts YHWH's military-cosmic sovereignty. Shiloh (šilôh H7887) was the central sanctuary during the judges period, housing the ark (Josh 18:1). Eli's sons Hophni and Phinehas are introduced in the same verse as the annual sacrifice — a narrative anticipation that these two are inseparable from the worship context that will expose their corruption.
4: The annual sacrifice generates mānōt (portions, H4490, from mānāh H4487, to count/apportion) distributed to family members. The portions represent the offerer's share in the peace offering (šĕlāmîm) eaten as communion with YHWH. Deut 12:11-12 mandates that the whole household — including servants — share in this meal. Elkanah's distribution sets up v5's tension: giving portions to Peninnah and her children (the fruitful wife) while reserving a special portion for Hannah (the barren one who is nevertheless loved) frames the conflict between social fruitfulness and covenantal love.
5: Elkanah gives Hannah mānāh ʾaḥat ʾappāyim — a double or choice portion; the exact phrase is unique and debated: 'double portion' (double of what the others received), or 'a portion of two faces/nostrils' (a sighing portion, i.e., accompanied by grief). The theological note — 'because YHWH had closed her womb' (kî-ʾāḥar rāḥam YHWH bĕʿaḏāh) — directly attributes Hannah's barrenness to divine action. This is consistent throughout the OT: YHWH 'closes' (Gen 20:18) and 'opens' (Gen 29:31; 30:22) wombs as sovereign acts. The attribution prevents a purely biological reading and frames Hannah's condition as a theological state awaiting divine resolution.
6: Peninnah is called Hannah's ṣārāh (H6869, rival/adversary — the technical term for a co-wife in a polygamous household; Lev 18:18 uses the root to regulate taking 'a woman as a rival to her sister'). Her kaʿas (H3707, provocation/vexing) is directed at Hannah 'because YHWH had closed her womb.' Peninnah weaponizes the evidence of divine action — Hannah's barrenness as visible sign of YHWH's withholding — as the basis for social mockery. YHWH's closing of the womb is thus made the catalyst for the prayer that will become the occasion of YHWH's opening it.
7: The pattern repeats annually (šānāh ḇĕšānāh): Hannah weeps and does not eat. Two standard markers of deep mourning in Hebrew culture (cf. David for Bathsheba's first son, 2 Sam 12:16; Ahab's sulking, 1 Kgs 21:4). The sanctuary visit — which Deut 16:11-14 commands as a time of rejoicing — becomes Hannah's annual moment of deepest grief. The gap between what YHWH commands (rejoice at the feast) and what YHWH has given her cause for (grief over barrenness) is the story's emotional architecture. The liturgical feast as the occasion of deepest personal anguish sets up prayer that is prayer indeed — not ritual performance but desperate petition.
8: Elkanah's consolation — 'Am I not better to you than ten sons?' — uses the number ten as the superlative-complete number (the ideal family size implying more than enough). His misreading: he interprets Hannah's grief as a relational deficit when it is a theological and social condition (barrenness is a divine closure, a social shame, a missing covenant blessing). What a husband's affection cannot supply — the covenant blessing of children — YHWH alone can give. Elkanah's genuine love and genuine misunderstanding together make Hannah's turn to YHWH in prayer the only remaining recourse.
9: Hannah rises (wayyāqom) after eating at Shiloh — textually debated; LXX suggests she rose before eating. mārah lāh (it was bitter to her) uses the root mārar (H4843, to be bitter) — the same root as Naomi's Mara (Ruth 1:20). The bitterness of soul (marāh nāpeš) is a recognized OT psychological-spiritual state (Job 3:20; 7:11). Eli is positioned at the doorpost of the sanctuary (mĕzûzat hêḵal YHWH): the threshold-guardian whose role is to mediate between worshipers and YHWH, but who will in this scene misread the most authentic prayer before him.
10: Waṯṯitpallēl ʿal-YHWH — she prayed to YHWH. pālal (H6419, to pray/intercede) in the reflexive Hithpael means to interpose oneself before God — the prayer-form that brings oneself before the divine presence. wāḇāḵōh ṯiḇkeh (weeping she wept) — the cognate infinitive absolute intensifies: her weeping was complete, sustained, total. The combination of prayer and intense weeping characterizes the OT's most urgent intercession (Ezra 10:1; Ps 6:6; Hezekiah in Isa 38:2-3). Hannah's prayer is the OT model for petition that combines explicit request, vow, and complete emotional disclosure before YHWH.
11: Hannah's vow (neder H5088) contains: (1) invocation of YHWH Ṣəḇāʾôt; (2) double infinitive absolute 'if you will indeed see' (rāʾōh tirʾeh) — emphatic appeal; (3) the covenant verb zāḵar (remember me — active covenant engagement, not mere recall: Gen 8:1 [Noah], Gen 30:22 [Rachel]); (4) 'not forget your servant'; (5) specific petition: 'give your servant a male child'; (6) reciprocal vow: 'I will give him to YHWH all the days of his life'; (7) Nazirite sign: 'a razor shall not come upon his head.' The Nazirite vow (Num 6:1-21) combined no wine, no razor, no corpse contact — Hannah specifies the razor. The Qumran Samuel text expands the conditions; the LXX adds wine.
12: The verb hirḇĕṯāh lĕhitpallēl (she multiplied/continued her prayer) — Eli watches (šōmēr, H8104) from his seat at the doorpost. Eli's position — ʿal-hakkissēʾ ʿal-mĕzûzat hêḵal YHWH (on the seat by the doorpost of the temple of YHWH) — places him at the threshold between outer and inner sanctuary, the liturgical guardian. The hêḵal (H1964) is the main hall of the Shiloh sanctuary, the term used for both the tabernacle's inner structure and later the Solomonic temple. Eli sees lip movement without sound — and misreads it.
13: Hannah's prayer is mĕḏabberet ʿal-libbāh (speaking upon her heart) — silent, lip-only prayer without audible voice. This is one of the OT's earliest descriptions of purely mental prayer (as opposed to spoken petition). Qôlāh lōʾ yiššāmēaʿ (her voice was not heard) — the silent petition addressed to YHWH alone, not audible to human witnesses, is a theological statement about the audience of prayer. Eli's misreading of silent devotion as public drunkenness is doubly ironic: the priest who fails to discipline his sons' public sacrilege condemns the woman whose private prayer is exemplary.
14: Eli's accusation — ʿaḏ-māṯay tiššəkārîn (how long will you be drunk?) — uses šāḵar (H7937, to be drunk). The irony: Eli's sons are guilty of cultic violations in the same sanctuary (2:13-17), yet the priest accuses the praying woman of drunkenness. His instruction — 'put away your wine' (hāsîrî ʾet-yênēḵ) — reads the external sign (moving lips, no sound) without perceiving the internal reality. This failure of spiritual discernment characterizes Eli throughout: he observes external forms while missing inner realities — the same failure that will make him unable to see YHWH's presence while Samuel receives it in the same room (ch3).
15: Hannah's self-defense: 'ʾiššāh qĕšat-rûaḥ ʾānōḵî' — I am a hard/pressed-spirit woman. qāšāh (H7186, hard/severe) applied to rûaḥ: her spirit is under severe pressure. 'I have poured out my soul before YHWH' — šāpaḵ ʾet-napšî lipnê YHWH: the metaphor of pouring out (šāpaḵ, H8210, to pour/spill out) the nepeš (soul/inner self/animating life) before YHWH captures the image of total self-disclosure in prayer. Used in Ps 42:4 ('I pour out my soul') and Lam 2:19 ('Pour out your heart like water before the face of the Lord'). The image treats the person as a vessel whose entire inner contents are emptied before YHWH in prayer.
16: 'Do not regard your servant as a daughter of Belial' — al-tittēn ʾet-ʾamaṯḵā lipnê bat-bĕliyyāʿal. bat-bĕliyyāʿal (daughter of worthlessness/Belial) — the feminine form of the descriptor the narrator will apply to Eli's sons in 2:12 (bĕnê bĕliyyāʿal). The irony is sharp: Eli is applying to the praying woman the exact characterization that correctly describes his own sons. Bĕliyyāʿal (H1100): 'without profit/worth' or 'without ascending' — a compound of bĕlî (without) + yaʿal (profit/value). She concludes: 'I have been speaking from the abundance of my complaint and grief' (mērōḇ śîḥî wĕḵaʿăśî) — śîaḥ (H7879, anxious musing/complaint) + kaʿas (H3707, vexation/grief) — the vocabulary of deep grief-prayer.
17: Eli's blessing — lĕḵî lĕšālôm (go in peace) — is the standard priestly benediction. 'The God of Israel grant your petition which you have asked of him' (wĕʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl yittēn ʾet-šĕlātēḵ ʾăšer šāʾalt) — the verb šāʾal (H7592, to ask/request) appears here for the first time in the chapter and will dominate vv18-28. The blessing is pronounced without Eli knowing the content of the petition — he is endorsing the form (earnest prayer) before knowing the substance. This is the priestly blessing functioning as a genuine covenant channel: the blessing pronounced in YHWH's name has efficacy regardless of the priest's knowledge of its specific application.
18: Hannah goes away, eats, and 'her face was no longer sad' (ûpānehā lōʾ-hāyû-lāh ʿôḏ) — before any empirical change in her condition. The change in countenance signals an inner resolution: she has transferred her burden to YHWH in prayer, and the priestly blessing has confirmed YHWH's receipt of it. This is the OT portrait of the faith that receives before the evidence — the peace that precedes the answer (cf. Phil 4:6-7: 'in everything by prayer and supplication with thanksgiving let your requests be made known to God, and the peace of God will guard your hearts'). The mechanism is the same: full petition → transferred burden → peace.
19: They rise early (wayyaškimû babboqer) and worship before YHWH — the morning worship follows the prayer-and-blessing sequence. 'And YHWH remembered her' (wayyizkor-āh YHWH) — zāḵar (H2142, to remember) is the covenant verb of divine initiative: YHWH 'remembered' Noah (Gen 8:1), Abraham (Gen 19:29), Rachel (Gen 30:22). YHWH's remembering is not the recovery of forgotten information but the active covenant engagement that initiates fulfillment. The verse's pairing of 'Elkanah knew Hannah his wife' with 'YHWH remembered her' places human and divine action in sequence: the means (normal marital relations) and the agency (YHWH's active covenant remembering) are both present.
20: Hannah conceives and bears a son named Šĕmûʾēl (Samuel H8050). The etymology given — 'because I asked him from YHWH' (kî mēYHWH šĕʾiltîw) — is based on šāʾal (to ask). The name's literal elements are more likely šēm (name) + ʾēl (God) = 'his name is God' or 'heard of God' — but the šāʾal etymology fits better the name Šāʾûl (Saul). This is a much-discussed lexical tension: the etymology that belongs to Saul's name is given to Samuel. Some scholars read this as deliberate — the man who embodies YHWH's 'asking' (the one truly given to YHWH) is Samuel, while the king Israel will 'ask for' (šāʾal) in ch8 is Saul. The šāʾal wordplay will saturate the chapter's conclusion.
21: Elkanah goes up to make the yearly sacrifice and pay his vow (ʾet-nidĕrô) — a vow not narrated previously, suggesting he also made a commitment at the Shiloh visit. Hannah decides not to go until Samuel is weaned — standard Iron Age practice was nursing for 2-3 years. The specific mention of Elkanah's vow suggests both spouses responded to Hannah's crisis with vows; the narrative focuses on Hannah's because it is the one that directly concerned Samuel's dedication. The transition sets up the tension: Hannah will fulfill her vow when the child is old enough to remain permanently at the sanctuary.
22: Hannah's stated intention — 'he may appear before YHWH and remain there forever' (wĕyāšaḇ šām ʿad-ʿôlām) — uses ʿôlām (H5769, forever/indefinitely). The same word governs YHWH's covenant promises (Abrahamic, Davidic). Hannah applies the covenant-permanence vocabulary to her son's service: not a temporary loan but a permanent dedication. Elkanah's response — 'only may YHWH establish his word' (yāqēm YHWH ʾet-dĕḇārô) — implies that YHWH has already spoken a word concerning Samuel (through the priestly blessing, or a direct oracle not narrated), and the proper response to fulfilling the vow is to invoke YHWH's confirmation of his own prior word.
23: Elkanah's phrase 'only may YHWH establish his word' — the LXX reads 'establish what proceeds from your mouth' (harmonizing to Hannah's vow), but MT's 'his word' (dĕḇārô) refers to a prior YHWH word about Samuel. If the priestly blessing of v17 is in view, Elkanah is invoking its fulfillment; alternatively, a direct divine communication not narrated here may be implied. Either way, Elkanah frames Hannah's vow-fulfillment within YHWH's own prior speech — the human vow-keeping and divine word-keeping are aligned as a single purpose-event.
24: When Samuel is weaned, Hannah brings him to Shiloh with a three-year-old bull (par mĕšulaš), an ephah of flour (ʾêpāh ʾaḥat qemeaḥ), and a skin of wine (nēḇel yāyin). The three-year-old bull is the peak-maturity sacrificial animal — Numbers 15:8-10 prescribes a bull for major vow-fulfillment offerings. The full sacrificial equipment (bull, flour, wine — the components of a grain offering with liquid accompaniment) signals that this is a vow-fulfillment offering of the highest grade. Wĕhannaʿar nāʿar (the boy was young) — the repetition of the naʿar root emphasizes his youth, setting up the contrast between his smallness and the gravity of the covenant act about to occur.
25: They slaughter the bull and bring the child to Eli — the sacrifice precedes the presentation. The sequence (sacrifice → bring to Eli → present to YHWH) follows the vow-fulfillment pattern: the sacrifice ratifies the vow; the priest receives the dedication; the child enters permanent sanctuary service. Samuel is being given not as Eli's personal servant but as YHWH's sanctuary servant — the distinction will become critical in ch2 when Samuel's faithful ministry contrasts with Eli's sons' corruption. The scene's formality emphasizes the covenant seriousness of the transfer: this is legally binding dedication, not a temporary lending.
26: Hannah's self-identification to Eli — 'I am the woman who was standing here in your presence, praying to YHWH' — uses the oath formula bî ʾăḏōnî ḥay napšĕḵā ('as your soul lives, my lord'). The anamnesis: 'I am the woman' (ʾānî hāʾiššāh) — the woman Eli could not correctly identify when he saw her praying now identifies herself in the light of what YHWH has done. The priest who called her drunk is looking at the answered prayer. The identification without reproach or triumph is itself a form of hesed: she returns in gratitude, not in score-settling.
27: 'For this child I prayed, and YHWH has granted me my petition which I asked of him' — wayyittēn YHWH lî ʾet-šĕʾēlātî ʾăšer šāʾaltî mēʿimmô. The verse concentrates the šāʾal vocabulary: šĕʾēlātî (my petition, what-I-asked) and šāʾaltî (I asked). The fulfillment of prayer is expressed as exact correspondence between petition and answer: she asked for this child, YHWH gave precisely what was asked. The verb nātan (gave) — YHWH as the giver who matches his gift to the specific request — is the theology of answered prayer (James 1:5: 'God gives generously to all without finding fault').
28: 'Therefore I have lent him to YHWH. As long as he lives, he is lent to YHWH' — wĕgam ʾānōḵî hišʾiltîhû laYHWH ... šāʾûl laYHWH. The final wordplay: hišʾiltîhû (I have asked/lent him out — the Hiphil of šāʾal) and šāʾûl laYHWH (he is asked-for/lent to YHWH — the passive participle). šāʾûl is precisely the name of Saul (the future king Israel will 'ask for' in ch8). Samuel ends ch1 described as šāʾûl laYHWH — the lexical shadow of the coming king falls over the closing of the story of the prophet who will anoint him. The chapter that is about Samuel's naming ends with the word that names Saul.
